Title: Leonard's Bakery
Description:
Malasada institution since 1952 — get the custard-filled haupia and eat them in the parking lot.

Category: bakery / hawaiian
Area: Kapahulu
Price: $
Must Order: haupia (coconut) malasada puff (the insider's pick over custard); original plain
Parking: Dedicated parking lot (~15–20 spots)

Founded in 1952 by Leonard Rego Jr., a second-generation Portuguese-Hawaiian whose family traced back to Madeira and the Azores sugar plantation immigrants. Malasadas were traditionally made on Shrove Tuesday (Fat Tuesday) to use up sugar and butter before Lent — Leonard turned the tradition into a year-round institution. The line stretches out the door from morning until close — 20-30 minute waits are standard, longer on weekends. The original plain malasada is perfect, but the haupia (coconut) puff is the insider pick. Bringing a box of Leonard's to a party, office, or someone's house is a power move in Honolulu — never refrigerate them.

Title: Liliha Bakery
Description:
Coco puffs since 1950 — the green chiffon cake and butter rolls are the real insider order.

Category: bakery / hawaiian
Area: Liliha
Price: $
Must Order: coco puffs (non-negotiable); butter rolls ($1)
Parking: Dedicated lot at the original Kuakini St location; Nimitz Hwy location has larger lot

Founded in 1950 by Takeo and Sumie Takakuwa, a Japanese-American couple who built a neighborhood bakery that became a Honolulu institution. The Coco Puff — choux pastry filled with chocolate pudding and topped with Chantilly frosting — is arguably Hawaii's most iconic pastry. Locals just say 'I brought puffs' and everyone on Oahu knows exactly what that means. A box is the standard omiyage (gift) when visiting someone's home. Go before 7am weekdays for fresh batches and zero line — the butter rolls for a dollar are the quiet insider pick.

Title: Nisshodo Candy Store
Description:
Handmade Japanese mochi and manju since the 1920s — hidden in an industrial warehouse, sold out by noon.

Category: bakery / mochi-shop
Area: Kalihi
Price: $
Must Order: chichi dango; mochi; manju
Parking: Free lot on Dillingham Blvd
*** CASH ONLY ***

The Asato family has been making Japanese mochi and manju by hand in this Kalihi warehouse since 1921, making Nisshodo over a century old. The chichi dango recipe has never changed -- glutinous rice flour, sugar, and potato starch, shaped by hand each morning. During New Year's and Obon season, orders back up for weeks. The industrial setting on Dillingham Blvd gives no hint of the treasure inside. Featured on multiple food shows but stubbornly unchanged by the attention.

Title: Canoes Surf Break (Waikiki)
Description:
The birthplace of modern surfing — gentle, long-rolling waves where Duke Kahanamoku and generations of locals learned to ride.

Category: beach / surfing
Area: Waikiki
Price: Free (board rentals $15–25/hr from beach boys on the sand)
Hours: Open daily, sunrise to sunset; best surf windows early morning and late afternoon
Best Time: Year-round — south swells (May–Sep) bring the most consistent waves; mornings before 8 AM for glassy conditions and fewer boards in the water
Parking: Waikiki Shell lot ($5), Kapiolani Park, or Honolulu Zoo lot; street parking is a lost cause
Kid Friendly: Yes

Canoes is named after the old canoe-surfing tradition — ancient Hawaiians rode these breaks in outrigger canoes centuries before fiberglass existed. Duke Kahanamoku grew up surfing here in the early 1900s and spread surfing to the world from this exact break. The wave peels slowly over a sandy bottom for 100+ yards, making it the most forgiving longboard wave on Oahu. The beach boys who work the sand in front of the Moana Surfrider have been giving lessons here since the 1920s — it's the longest-running surf school tradition on Earth. Pro tip: paddle out at the channel between Canoes and Queens to avoid the whitewater crowd, and stay to the left of the break where the wave is mushiest and most forgiving. Crowded by 10 AM with rental boards, so dawn patrol is your friend.

Title: Hanauma Bay
Description:
A protected marine sanctuary inside a volcanic crater — the snorkeling is like swimming in an aquarium.

Category: beach / snorkeling
Area: East Honolulu
Price: $25 non-resident, $5 kama'aina; closed Mondays and Tuesdays
Hours: Wed–Sun, 6:45 AM – 4:00 PM (gates close at 1:30 PM for last entry)
Best Time: Book online reservation immediately when slots open (2 days in advance); Wed or Thu for fewest crowds
Parking: $3 parking; lot fills by 9 AM, arrive early or take the shuttle
Kid Friendly: Yes

Hanauma means 'curved bay' in Hawaiian — it formed inside a volcanic crater that eroded on one side, letting the ocean flood in thousands of years ago. Elvis Presley filmed scenes from Blue Hawaii here in 1961, cementing its fame. The mandatory conservation video before entry is no gimmick; the bay nearly died from overuse in the 1990s before strict limits brought the reef back. Go before 7 AM on a Wednesday for the emptiest experience, bring your own gear, and stay in the inner reef where 400+ fish species are so tame they brush against you.

Title: Kailua Beach Park
Description:
The beach Honolulu locals make the drive for — powder-white sand, gentle clear water, views of the Mokes.

Category: beach / swimming
Area: Kailua
Price: Free
Hours: Open daily, 5:00 AM – 10:00 PM
Best Time: Arrive before 9am weekends; weekday mornings are uncrowded
Parking: 3 free lots — arrive before 9am on weekends
Kid Friendly: Yes

Kailua means 'two seas' or 'two currents' in Hawaiian, referring to the twin channels flanking the bay. President Obama repeatedly vacationed here, putting this once-sleepy local beach on the global map. Kayak to Moku Nui (the larger Mokulua Island) and hike over the ridge to a hidden back-side beach with zero people. The afternoon trade winds turn the bay into one of the world's best kiteboarding spots — even if you don't kite, the aerial show from shore is worth staying for.

Title: Lanikai Beach
Description:
Consistently ranked among the world's most beautiful — turquoise water so clear it looks fake.

Category: beach / swimming
Area: Kailua
Price: Free
Hours: Open 24 hours (public beach access via neighborhood paths)
Best Time: Early morning for calm water and fewer people; sunrise is spectacular
Parking: No public lot — park at Kailua Beach Park and walk 10 min
Kid Friendly: Yes

Lanikai means 'heavenly sea' in Hawaiian — a name coined in the 1920s when the area was first developed as a residential neighborhood. There is no public parking lot, no restrooms, and no lifeguards by design; residents fought to keep it that way. Access is through narrow blue-signed paths between beachfront homes on Mokulua Drive that are easy to miss. Do the Lanikai Pillbox hike at sunrise first — the aerial view of the Mokulua Islands from the WWII bunkers above is one of Oahu's most iconic sights, then cool off with a swim below.

Title: Waikiki Beach
Description:
Yes, it's touristy — but the surf break is gentle, the people-watching is elite, and the sunset with Diamond Head is undeniable.

Category: beach / swimming
Area: Waikiki
Price: Free
Hours: Open 24 hours
Best Time: Early morning for calm water and space; sunset for the iconic view
Parking: Use the Waikiki Shell lot ($5) or park at Kapiolani Park; street parking is near impossible
Kid Friendly: Yes

Waikiki means 'spouting waters' in Hawaiian, named for the freshwater springs that once fed taro fields and fishponds where hotels now stand. Duke Kahanamoku, the father of modern surfing and Olympic gold medalist, grew up surfing these breaks and his bronze statue on the beach is the most photographed landmark in Hawaii. The break called 'Canoes' is where generations of locals learned to surf — go at dawn before the rental longboard armada launches. For a quieter stretch, walk east past the Natatorium toward Diamond Head where the sand widens and the crowds thin out.

Title: Waimea Bay
Description:
Birthplace of big wave surfing in winter, glass-calm swimming hole in summer — two completely different worlds.

Category: beach / swimming
Area: North Shore
Price: Free
Hours: Open daily, 7:00 AM – 7:45 PM
Best Time: Summer for calm swimming and Jump Rock; winter for watching 30+ ft waves (do NOT swim)
Parking: Parking fills by 10am — arrive early or use Haleiwa shuttle

Waimea means 'reddish water' in Hawaiian, from the red soil washing into the bay. This is the birthplace of big-wave surfing — Greg Noll rode the first massive waves here in 1957, and the Eddie Aikau Big Wave Invitational, held only when swells hit 30+ feet, is surfing's most sacred event. Jump Rock on the left side is a summer rite of passage for local kids. In winter the bay becomes a churning death trap — even wading is deadly. Waimea Valley across the road makes a perfect combo day.

Title: Coffee Gallery
Description:
North Shore roastery with Oahu, Maui, and Kona beans — the real locals' coffee stop in Haleiwa.

Category: coffee / roaster
Area: Haleiwa
Price: $
Must Order: Waialua estate coffee; bag of Waialua beans ($12–16); iced mocha
Parking: Parking in the North Shore Marketplace lot — easy

A Haleiwa fixture since the early 2000s, Coffee Gallery roasts beans from multiple Hawaiian estates in-house: Waialua (grown right here on the North Shore), Kona, Maui, and Kauai. The Waialua estate coffee is the insider move — you’re drinking coffee grown 10 miles away on former Dole plantation land. Grab a bag of Waialua beans as a souvenir most visitors don’t know exists — Oahu-grown coffee is genuinely rare.

Title: Kona Coffee Purveyors
Description:
100% Kona paired with SF's b.Patisserie pastries — the best coffee in Waikiki, full stop.

Category: coffee / roaster
Area: Waikiki
Price: $$
Must Order: 100% Kona pour-over ($8–10); iced Kona latte; b.Patisserie pastries
Parking: Validated parking at International Market Place garage (3 hrs free with purchase)

Founded by Brandon Damitz, who personally visits every Big Island farm he sources from and knows the elevation, varietal, and processing method of every bean. He partnered with Belinda Leong of b.Patisserie — a James Beard-nominated SF bakery — so the kouign-amann here is world-class. The upstairs International Market Place location means most tourists walk right past. Go before 9am and ask which estate the current pour-over is from.

Title: Byodo-In Temple
Description:
A full-scale replica of a 900-year-old Kyoto temple tucked against the Ko'olau cliffs — ring the 3-ton bell for blessings.

Category: cultural / temple
Area: Kaneohe
Price: $
Hours: Daily 8:30am–4:30pm. Last entry 4:15pm
Best Time: Early morning for misty mountain backdrop and fewer crowds — golden hour light is stunning for photos
Parking: Free parking at the entrance
Kid Friendly: Yes

This 1968 replica of the 950-year-old Byodo-In in Uji, Japan was built entirely without nails using traditional Japanese joinery to honor a century of Japanese immigration to Hawaii. The 3-ton brass bon-sho bell at the entrance was cast in Osaka — ring it before entering to purify your mind. The 18-foot Amida Buddha inside is covered in gold lacquer and is among the largest carved wooden Buddhas outside Japan. Filming location for Lost, Hawaii Five-0, and Magnum P.I. — the surrounding Valley of the Temples is an active multi-faith cemetery, so be respectful.

Title: Iolani Palace
Description:
The only royal palace on American soil — home to Hawaii's last monarchs, with electricity before the White House.

Category: cultural / historical-site
Area: Downtown
Price: $27
Hours: Tue-Sat 9am-4pm; tours at set times
Best Time: Morning guided tours; Friday noon concerts by the Royal Hawaiian Band on the grounds (free)
Parking: Metered street parking; paid lots on King St
Kid Friendly: Yes

Completed in 1882 for King Kalakaua, Iolani had electric lights four years before the White House and telephones before Buckingham Palace. After the 1893 overthrow, Queen Liliuokalani was imprisoned upstairs for eight months — you can see the quilt she sewed during confinement. The palace fell into severe disrepair and a massive restoration beginning in 1969 recovered original furnishings from basements across the state. The koa wood staircase alone took artisans years to restore.

Title: Kukaniloko Birthing Stones
Description:
The most sacred site on Oahu — where ali'i (royalty) were born for over 700 years, radiating mana from the island's center.

Category: cultural / sacred-site
Area: Wahiawa
Price: Free
Hours: Open daily, sunrise to sunset
Best Time: Early morning when you may have it to yourself — the silence adds to the gravity of the place
Parking: Small dirt lot off Whitmore Ave — look for the state historical marker
Kid Friendly: Yes

For over 700 years, ali'i (royal) women traveled to this site in the geographic center of Oahu to give birth, attended by 36 chiefs who served as witnesses to validate the child's royal lineage. The petroglyphs carved into the stones are among the oldest on Oahu, and the eucalyptus-lined path you walk today follows what was once a ceremonial approach. The stones are believed to hold immense mana — do not sit on or touch them, and do not leave offerings unless guided by a cultural practitioner. Most locals consider this the single most sacred place on the island; treat it accordingly.

Title: Pearl Harbor National Memorial
Description:
The USS Arizona Memorial, Battleship Missouri, and the site where December 7, 1941 changed the world — free admission, book ahead.

Category: cultural / historic
Area: Pearl Harbor
Price: Free
Hours: Daily 7am–5pm. Last Arizona Memorial boat at 3:30pm. Closed Thanksgiving, Christmas, New Year's Day
Best Time: Arrive by 7am for walk-up tickets or book online 8 weeks ahead on Recreation.gov ($1 service fee). Mornings are cooler and less crowded
Parking: $7/day in visitor center lot — arrives early, fills fast. Do NOT leave valuables in your car (high-theft area)
Kid Friendly: Yes

The USS Arizona still leaks about a quart of oil per day — sailors call them 'black tears,' and the slick is visible from the memorial platform. The structure was designed by Alfred Preis, an Austrian-born architect who was himself interned as an enemy alien after the attack. Its dip-and-rise shape symbolizes initial defeat and ultimate victory. Arrive by 7am for walk-up tickets or book on Recreation.gov eight weeks ahead. NO BAGS ALLOWED — only wallets, phones, cameras, and water bottles. Plan a full day if visiting all four Pearl Harbor sites.

Title: Hans Hedemann Surf School
Description:
Founded by a former Pipeline pro — the gold standard for Waikiki surf lessons with guaranteed stand-ups and zero bro energy.

Category: experience / surf-school
Area: Waikiki
Price: $$ — Group lesson ~$80, semi-private ~$135, private ~$200 (2 hrs, board included)
Hours: Daily 8 AM–4 PM; lessons every 2 hours
Best Time: 8 AM first session for smallest crowds and cleanest waves; book 2–3 days ahead in peak season
Parking: Park at Waikiki Shell lot or Kapiolani Park; school meets at the Queen Kapiolani Hotel, 150 Kapahulu Ave
Kid Friendly: Yes

Hans Hedemann grew up on Oahu, was ranked 3rd in the world as a pro surfer, competed at Pipeline starting in 1978, and starred in the 1987 surf film North Shore before founding his surf school around 2001. His instructors are all certified watermen — not random backpackers — and the school has maintained the highest safety record in Waikiki for over two decades. They teach at Canoes and Queens breaks using soft-top longboards and a patient, methodical approach. The 4:1 student-to-instructor ratio means you actually get coached, not just pushed into whitewater and abandoned. They also run an advanced program for intermediates wanting to progress to shortboards. Located inside the Queen Kapiolani Hotel lobby at 150 Kapahulu Ave, which means real changing rooms and showers — a luxury most surf schools skip.

Title: Uncle Bryan's Sunset Suratt Surf School
Description:
A born-and-raised North Shore uncle who teaches surfing the way it was meant to be learned — with aloha, patience, and deep ocean knowledge.

Category: experience / surf-school
Area: Haleiwa
Price: $$ — Group lesson ~$85, private ~$175 (2 hrs, board and rash guard included)
Hours: Daily 9 AM–5 PM; lessons at 9 AM, 11 AM, 1 PM, 3 PM
Best Time: Summer (May–Sep) for the most beginner-friendly conditions on the North Shore; 9 AM session for glassiest water
Parking: Meet at Haleiwa Beach Park; free parking lot usually has space
Kid Friendly: Yes

Bryan Suratt grew up surfing Haleiwa and Sunset Beach and has been teaching since the 1990s. His school is the real deal — no fancy marketing, no hotel lobby, just an uncle with decades of wave knowledge who genuinely loves putting people on their first wave. Lessons happen at Puaena Point and Haleiwa Beach Park where the waves are forgiving and the vibe is local. Bryan's instructors are all North Shore locals who grew up in the water, and they teach ocean safety and respect alongside technique. You'll learn to read currents, understand wave sets, and position yourself — not just pop up and fall. Many local families send their keiki here for summer surf camps. The school also runs intermediate sessions at Chun's Reef for those ready to graduate from whitewater.

Title: KCC Farmers Market
Description:
Hawaii's biggest and best farmers market — 80+ vendors, incredible prepared food stalls, and a Diamond Head backdrop.

Category: farmers-market / weekend-market
Area: Kapahulu / Diamond Head
Price: $-$$
Hours: Saturdays 7:30am-11am; Tuesdays 4-7pm (smaller)
Best Time: Arrive at 7:30am Saturday opening; by 9:30am the best items sell out
Parking: Free parking at KCC campus; fills up fast
Kid Friendly: Yes

Founded in 2003 by the Hawaii Farm Bureau at Kapiolani Community College, KCC grew from a handful of tables into Hawaii's largest farmers market with 80+ vendors. Chef Ed Kenney and other local restaurant icons helped build its farm-to-table reputation by shopping here publicly. The Saturday market is a pilgrimage -- arrive at 7:30am opening or face the crowds. The Tuesday evening version is a local secret with the same vendors but a fraction of the Saturday mob.

Title: Diamond Head Summit
Description:
Oahu's most iconic crater climb — tunnels, stairs, and 360-degree views that justify the hype.

Category: hike / sunrise
Area: Diamond Head
Price: $
Hours: Gate opens 6am; last entry 4pm
Best Time: Sunrise — book the 6am slot weeks in advance; it sells out
Parking: $10/vehicle inside the crater; $5/person entry (reservations required)
Kid Friendly: Yes

Hawaiians called it Le'ahi ('brow of the ahi fish') for its profile — British sailors in 1825 mistook calcite crystals for diamonds and the English name stuck. The US Army built Fort Ruger here in 1908, carving the tunnels and fire control station you climb through today as a coastal defense post to spot enemy ships. The spiral staircase and dark tunnel were part of the military observation network. Book reservations at gostateparks.hawaii.gov — the 6am sunrise slot sells out weeks ahead.

Title: Ka'ena Point Trail
Description:
Oahu's raw western tip — monk seals laze on rocks and albatross nest where the island ends.

Category: hike / coastal
Area: Waianae
Price: Free
Hours: Sunrise to sunset daily (no gates on the Waianae side)
Best Time: Early morning to beat the heat; Nov–Jun for albatross nesting season
Parking: Free lot at end of Farrington Hwy past Yokohama Bay

In Hawaiian mythology Ka'ena Point is a leina a ka 'uhane — a sacred 'leaping place of souls' where spirits of the dead depart the physical world into the afterlife realm of Po. The trail follows an abandoned railroad bed built in 1899 by Benjamin Dillingham's Oahu Railway & Land Co., which once connected Honolulu to the North Shore around this rugged coast. The fenced sanctuary protects nesting Laysan albatross (moli) and critically endangered Hawaiian monk seals. Bring 2+ liters of water — zero shade for 5 miles, and the currents here are deadly.

Title: Koko Crater Railway
Description:
1,048 railway ties straight up a crater — the most brutal 1.6-mile workout on the island.

Category: hike / challenging
Area: Hawaii Kai
Price: Free
Hours: Open 24 hours (no gates; parking lot at Koko Head District Park always accessible)
Best Time: Pre-dawn start (bring headlamp) — avoid midday heat at all costs
Parking: Free parking at Koko Head District Park

The 1,048 railroad ties were laid during WWII as a tramway to haul supplies and personnel to a radar tracking station at the summit — the military needed eyes on the ocean approaches after Pearl Harbor. The ties were originally fitted with rails and a cable car system. The notorious bridge section near the top spans a gap where the original trestle crossed a ravine. Inside the crater at the summit sits a forgotten botanical garden planted in the 1950s with plumeria, cacti, and native dryland species. Locals do this as a pre-dawn workout — arrive with a headlamp.

Title: Lanikai Pillbox
Description:
Two WWII bunkers over the Mokulua Islands — the most photogenic sunrise hike on the windward side.

Category: hike / pillbox
Area: Kailua
Price: Free
Hours: Sunrise to sunset (no gates; unofficial trail on public land)
Best Time: Sunrise — start 30 min before for the climb; the light over the Mokes is unbeatable
Parking: Street parking on Kaelepulu Dr — arrive before sunrise to find a spot

Officially the Ka'iwa Ridge Trail, these concrete bunkers were built after Pearl Harbor as coastal defense against Japanese invasion — soldiers watched for enemy ships from these exact vantage points. The name Lanikai ('heavenly sea') was coined in the 1920s when the area was subdivided. The Mokulua Islands offshore are state seabird sanctuaries. Start 30 minutes before sunrise for the famous golden light that makes this Oahu's most photographed hike.

Title: Makapu'u Lighthouse Trail
Description:
Fully paved and stroller-friendly with whale sightings Nov–May and dramatic ocean cliffs.

Category: hike / coastal
Area: Makapu'u
Price: Free
Hours: Sunrise to sunset daily (parking lot gate opens at sunrise)
Best Time: Jan–Mar for peak whale watching; early morning to avoid heat and crowds
Parking: Free lot off Kalanianaole Hwy (fills by 9am weekends)
Kid Friendly: Yes

The lighthouse at the summit has been guiding ships since 1909 and houses one of the largest lighthouse lenses in the country — a hyper-radiant Fresnel lens over 8 feet tall, shipped from Paris. The trail was originally built as an access road for lighthouse keepers who lived at the point. During whale season (Jan-Mar), this is the single best land-based whale watching spot in Hawaii — humpbacks breach so close you can hear the splash. The tidepools below the lookout harbor rare species, but only scramble down when surf is calm. Fully paved and stroller-accessible.

Title: Manoa Falls
Description:
A 150-ft jungle waterfall 15 minutes from Waikiki — Jurassic Park vibes are not an accident.

Category: hike / waterfall
Area: Manoa
Price: $
Hours: Open daily 8am–6pm (last entry by 4:30pm); closed during flash flood warnings
Best Time: Early morning for fewer crowds; after light rain for a fuller waterfall (but trail gets muddier)
Parking: $7 parking at 3739 Manoa Rd (the only lot)
Kid Friendly: Yes

Manoa Valley receives over 150 inches of rain annually, making it one of the wettest inhabited places in the US — that's why the 150-ft falls almost never dry up. Parts of Jurassic Park, Lost, and dozens of other productions filmed in this valley, and the ancient Hawaiians considered it home to Kahalaopuna, a beautiful maiden born of the wind and rain of Manoa. The trail follows a route once used by Hawaiian farmers tending taro and banana plots in the upper valley. Do NOT swim at the base — rockfall and leptospirosis are genuine risks.

Title: Bishop Museum
Description:
Hawaii's biggest museum with a world-class planetarium and a hands-on science center that rivals the mainland.

Category: kids / museum
Area: Kalihi
Price: $$
Hours: Wed–Mon 9am–5pm. Closed Tuesdays
Best Time: Arrive at opening on a weekday; planetarium shows sell out so grab tickets first
Parking: Free parking on-site in museum lot
Kid Friendly: Yes

Founded in 1889 by Charles Reed Bishop in honor of his wife Princess Bernice Pauahi Bishop, this is the largest museum in Polynesia and houses over 24 million artifacts including the world's foremost collection of Hawaiian and Pacific Island natural history. The Science Adventure Center's erupting volcano model was designed by the same firm behind Smithsonian exhibits. Most visitors miss the native Hawaiian garden behind the main hall and the original koa-wood Hawaiian Hall ceilings.

Title: Kualoa Ranch
Description:
Jurassic Valley ATV tours, horseback rides, and ziplines on a working cattle ranch — where they filmed Jurassic Park.

Category: kids / adventure
Area: Kualoa
Price: $$$
Hours: Daily 7:30am–5:30pm; tours run on set schedules — book online in advance
Best Time: Morning tours have better light for Kualoa Valley photos and cooler temps
Parking: Free parking on-site
Kid Friendly: Yes

This 4,000-acre working cattle ranch has been in the Morgan family since 1850, purchased from King Kamehameha III. The valley is considered one of the most sacred places on Oahu in Hawaiian tradition. Over 200 movies and TV shows have filmed here including Jurassic Park, Lost, and Hawaii Five-0. The 'Secret Island' beach -- included with some packages -- has calm shallow water and kayaks, the best-kept secret for families with young children.

Title: Polynesian Cultural Center
Description:
Six island villages, canoe rides, fire-knife dancing, and a luau — the best cultural experience on Oahu for families.

Category: kids / museum
Area: Laie
Price: $$$
Hours: Mon–Sat 12:30pm–9pm. Closed Sundays
Best Time: Arrive at opening to see all six villages before the evening show; weekdays for smaller crowds
Parking: Free parking on-site
Kid Friendly: Yes

Founded in 1963, the center was originally staffed by BYU-Hawaii students who performed and demonstrated crafts to pay tuition -- a tradition continuing today with over 100 student employees from Pacific Island nations. Six villages represent Hawaii, Samoa, Tonga, Fiji, Tahiti, and Aotearoa. The evening 'Ha: Breath of Life' show features a 100-person cast with the world-famous Samoan fire-knife dance. Arrive at 12:30 sharp to start with the least-crowded village.

Title: Tamashiro Market
Description:
Honolulu's most trusted fish market for over 80 years — where local families buy poke and fresh seafood for parties.

Category: market / fish-market
Area: Kalihi
Price: $$
Must Order: shoyu ahi poke; spicy tako poke; poke plate
Parking: Small lot in front; street parking on N King St

The Tamashiro family opened this Kalihi fish market in the 1940s, originally serving the neighborhood's Japanese and Filipino fishing families. The poke counter -- with a dozen daily varieties of ahi, tako, and seasonal specials -- is arguably the best on Oahu. The hand-painted exterior murals have become a Kalihi landmark. Come Saturday before 10am for the freshest selection; by noon, the popular cuts are gone and locals are eating styrofoam plates standing in the parking lot.

Title: Bar Leather Apron
Description:
One of the world's best cocktail bars hidden on a downtown Honolulu mezzanine — 15 seats, Japanese technique, local ingredients.

Category: nightlife / cocktail-bar
Area: Downtown
Price: $$$
Must Order: bartender's choice cocktail; old fashioned with Hawaiian honey
Parking: Street parking; paid lot nearby

Founded by Justin Park, who left a finance career to pursue bartending, this 15-seat speakeasy landed on Asia's 50 Best Bars and won a Spirited Award at Tales of the Cocktail. Justin trained in Tokyo and brings Japanese precision to every drink, using house-made syrups from local citrus and Hawaiian honey. Reservations are essential and released weekly online -- walk-ins only work on quiet Tuesday or Wednesday nights.

Title: La Mariana Sailing Club
Description:
Hawaii's original tiki bar since 1957 — hidden in an industrial marina with museum-worthy tiki artifacts and strong mai tais.

Category: nightlife / tiki-bar
Area: Sand Island
Price: $$
Must Order: mai tai; blue hawaii
Parking: Free lot at the marina

Annette Nahinu La Mariana opened this marina bar in 1957 and spent decades rescuing tiki artifacts from demolished restaurants -- puffer fish lamps from Trader Vic's original Oakland location, rattan from Don the Beachcomber, carvings from the Hilton's Tapa Room. She passed away in 2012 but her irreplaceable collection remains intact. Quentin Tarantino and Bill Murray have both made pilgrimages here. The Friday night live music with ukulele singalongs feels frozen in 1962.

Title: Fête
Description:
Hawaii's first James Beard Best Chef winner in 19 years — seasonal New American meets local soul food in a Chinatown bistro.

Category: restaurant / fine-dining
Area: Chinatown / Downtown
Price: $$$
Must Order: carbonara with Portuguese sausage; twice-fried chicken with grits and collard greens; duck fat fries
Parking: Street parking on Smith/Bethel; paid lots on Maunakea St

Opened 2016 by Chef Robynne Maii — won the James Beard Award for Best Chef Northwest & Pacific in 2022, the first Hawaii-based chef to win the regional award since Alan Wong in 2003. The menu bridges Southern comfort, French technique, and Hawaiian ingredients. Mon–Sat 11am–10pm. Ask for a seat near the open kitchen. Great combined with a Chinatown walk — galleries, Maunakea Marketplace, and Manifest coffee/cocktails all nearby.

Title: Helena's Hawaiian Food
Description:
James Beard Award winner in a Kalihi strip mall — the pipikaula short ribs will ruin you for all other beef.

Category: restaurant / hawaiian
Area: Kalihi
Price: $$
Must Order: Hawaiian plate (pipikaula short ribs, lomi salmon, poi, haupia)
Parking: Strip mall parking lot on N. School St in Kalihi

Founded in 1946 by Helen 'Helena' Chock in a Kalihi strip mall, Helena's won the James Beard America's Classics Award in 2000 — the only traditional Hawaiian restaurant ever honored and a landmark moment for indigenous cuisine recognition. Now run by her grandson Craig Katsuyoshi, the pipikaula short ribs (Hawaiian-style dried beef) are the signature you cannot skip. Cash only, limited hours (Tue-Fri), and the line moves slow — but this is sacred-level Hawaiian food. Eat poi with two fingers, never one, never a fork.

Title: Highway Inn Kakaako
Description:
Traditional Hawaiian plate lunch since 1947 — the poi, laulau, and pipikaula are as real as it gets.

Category: restaurant / hawaiian
Area: Kakaako
Price: $$
Must Order: Laulau — pork and butterfish wrapped in taro and ti leaves, steamed tender; Pipikaula short ribs — Hawaiian-style dried beef, grilled smoky and chewy; Poi — fresh-pounded, the real deal, order it with your plate
Parking: Parking lot at SALT at Our Kakaako. Some street parking on Coral St.

Founded in 1947 by Seiichi and Nancy Toguchi on the old Waipahu highway connecting sugar plantation camps — hence the name. Now in its fourth generation under Monica Toguchi, who opened the sleek Kakaako location in SALT while keeping the original Waipahu spot alive. The poi is fresh-pounded, the laulau uses traditional taro and ti leaf wrapping, and the pipikaula is smoky perfection. A James Beard semifinalist, Highway Inn bridges old Hawaiian traditions with a modern setting without compromising the recipes.

Title: Ono Seafood
Description:
Made-to-order poke that locals will wait 30 minutes for — the shoyu ahi is non-negotiable.

Category: restaurant / poke
Area: Kapahulu
Price: $
Must Order: shoyu ahi ($14–16); spicy ahi
Parking: Only 5–6 parking spots behind shop; park on side streets off Kapahulu

Founded by the Ono family in 1995 on Kapahulu Ave — 'ono' means 'delicious' in Hawaiian, and the name delivers. They cut and season fish to order, never pre-mixed, using a shoyu-sesame marinade recipe that hasn't changed in nearly 30 years. Featured on countless 'Best Poke' lists including Travel Channel, Food Network, and Bon Appetit. The tiny shop has no seating — the local move is to walk your bowl to Kapiolani Park for a Diamond Head view picnic. Sells out by early afternoon most days.

Title: Palace Saimin
Description:
Hand-wrapped won tons since 1946 — if this place closes, Honolulu loses its soul.

Category: restaurant / hawaiian
Area: Kalihi
Price: $
Must Order: won ton min ($9–12, hand-wrapped won tons in pork-shrimp broth); teriyaki beef sticks ($2–4 each — NON-NEGOTIABLE side order)
Parking: Small lot next to the building on King Street
*** CASH ONLY ***

Family-run since 1946. Saimin is Hawaii's own noodle soup — evolved on sugar plantations where Japanese, Chinese, Filipino, and Portuguese workers shared ingredients. The tiny dining room packs out during lunch and dinner — lines form outside on weekends and evenings. Open Tue–Fri 10am–8pm, Sat 10am–3pm. Closed Sun and Mon. Combine with Helena's and Mitsu-Ken (also in Kalihi) for a full local food pilgrimage.

Title: Rainbow Drive-In
Description:
The plate lunch cathedral since 1961 — mixed plate with gravy is the move.

Category: restaurant / plate-lunch
Area: Kapahulu
Price: $
Must Order: mixed plate with gravy ($12–14) — teriyaki beef + mahimahi + fried chicken, 2 scoops rice, mac salad
Parking: Small lot in front, fills up fast at lunch — street parking on Kanaina Ave

Founded in 1961 by Seiju Ifuku, a WWII veteran of the legendary 442nd Regimental Combat Team — the most decorated unit in US military history, composed entirely of Japanese-Americans from Hawaii. 'Plate lunch' evolved from plantation-era bento boxes; Rainbow helped codify the modern two-scoop-rice format. Barack Obama ate here regularly growing up and during presidential visits. The line snakes through the parking lot during the noon rush — order before 11:30am or after 1pm to skip the worst of it. The mixed plate with gravy over rice is the canonical order. Take your plate one block to Kapiolani Park and eat under a monkeypod tree facing Diamond Head.

Title: Senia
Description:
Three-Michelin-star trained chef running two experiences under one roof — casual a la carte downstairs, elite 8-seat Chef's Counter upstairs.

Category: restaurant / fine-dining
Area: Chinatown / Downtown
Price: $$$
Must Order: Chef's Counter tasting menu (Fri/Sat only, reserve weeks ahead); seared foie gras with haupia; any dessert — pastry chef Michelle Karr-Ueoka trained at Per Se and French Laundry
Parking: Street parking on King/Nuuanu; paid lots on Smith St

Opened in 2016 by Chef Chris Kajioka and pastry chef Michelle Karr-Ueoka, both Alan Wong alumni. Kajioka trained at Per Se under Thomas Keller and at Vintage Cave. The 8-seat Chef's Counter (Fri-Sat only) is a multi-course tasting menu ranked among Hawaii's best dining experiences — book 3-4 weeks ahead. Won Hale 'Aina Gold for Best Restaurant and Best Tasting Menu multiple years. The a la carte dining room downstairs is more approachable for a weeknight. Michelle's desserts draw on French Laundry precision with local Hawaiian flavors.

Title: Side Street Inn
Description:
Where Honolulu chefs eat on their day off — the fried rice feeds four and costs sixteen bucks.

Category: restaurant / plate-lunch
Area: Ala Moana
Price: $$
Must Order: pan-fried pork chops ($16–20, THE signature dish); signature fried rice ($14–16, enormous portions)
Parking: Street parking

Colin Nishida opened Side Street Inn in 1992 as a neighborhood bar, then his pan-fried pork chops got so famous he earned a James Beard semifinalist nod. After Honolulu's top chefs close their own kitchens at 10-11pm, they come here — this is literally where the restaurant industry eats. Anthony Bourdain visited and called it essential. Order family-style, 3-4 dishes for two people. The fried rice portion feeds a small army for $16.

Title: Tokkuri Tei
Description:
Honolulu's original izakaya — the garlic ahi belly and tofu steak are why reservations fill a week out.

Category: restaurant / izakaya
Area: Kapahulu
Price: $$
Must Order: ika yaki (squid pancake, $12–14 — THE signature dish); maguro tataki ($14–16); garlic edamame; nasu dengaku (miso eggplant); black sesame ice cream
Parking: Street parking on side streets mauka of Kapahulu

Opened in the early 1990s by chef/owner Hide Yoshimoto (Hide-san), Tokkuri Tei was one of the first authentic izakayas in Hawaii and helped spark the island's entire izakaya boom. The ika yaki (squid pancake) is the legendary signature dish. Order multiple small plates to share — izakaya style means rounds of dishes, not one entree per person. Ask staff for sake pairing guidance. Go Tue/Wed for walk-in seating; weekends book up fast.

Title: Zippy's Restaurant
Description:
Hawaii's diner chain that every local grew up on — chili and zip packs are a way of life.

Category: restaurant / plate-lunch
Area: King Street
Price: $
Must Order: Zippy's Chili — the iconic thick, beefy chili that's been the same since 1966; Zip Pack — fried chicken, chili, rice, and mac salad in one legendary combo; Napoleon Bakery cream puffs — the grab-and-go bakery case is dangerously good
Parking: Most locations have their own lot. King Street location has a parking lot behind the building.

Founded in 1966 by Francis Higa and Charles Toguchi, Zippy's is Hawaii's most beloved diner chain with 20+ locations across Oahu. The chili recipe is a legit state secret — they've never revealed it in nearly 60 years. Every local kid grew up eating here after practice, after prom, at 2am. The Zip Pack (fried chicken, chili, rice, mac salad) is an institution. Napoleon Bakery inside sells cakes and cream puffs for every birthday party on the island. It's not fancy, but for locals it's home.

Title: Island Snow
Description:
Obama's Kailua go-to — every presidential visit = a stop here. Also a legit surf shop.

Category: shave-ice / classic
Area: Kailua
Price: $
Must Order: any flavor with vanilla ice cream on the bottom (the Island Snow signature); li hing mui powder on top; passion orange guava (POG)
Parking: Parking in the Kailua Shopping Center lot next door

A Kailua institution since 1979, Island Snow was a legit surf and skate shop long before Barack Obama made it famous. Every Christmas vacation during his presidency, Obama's motorcade would pull up for shave ice -- Secret Service and all. Owner Craig Sugihara sponsors local surf groms and the shop is deeply woven into Kailua's beach culture. The signature move is a scoop of vanilla ice cream buried at the bottom of every cone, which you discover as the shave ice melts down.

Title: Matsumoto Shave Ice
Description:
The North Shore icon since 1951 — the rainbow combo is non-negotiable.

Category: shave-ice / classic
Area: Haleiwa
Price: $
Must Order: rainbow combo (strawberry-lemon-pineapple); lilikoi; li hing mui; coconut; azuki beans + ice cream on the bottom
Parking: Street parking — fills fast on weekends

Mamoru and Helen Matsumoto opened this Haleiwa general store in 1951, originally selling groceries to plantation workers. The shave ice was just a side offering, but it became so popular that by the 1970s it had taken over the entire business. The original wooden building and hand-painted sign are now North Shore landmarks. Lines wrap around the building during peak season — 20 minutes on a good day, 45-60 minutes when tour buses arrive. Arrive right at 10am opening for the shortest wait. President Obama stopped here during Hawaii vacations, and the branded T-shirts have become a legitimate Oahu souvenir. Over a thousand customers per day during peak season.

Title: Uncle Clay's House of Pure Aloha
Description:
All-natural syrups and real fruit — this is shave ice elevated to an art form.

Category: shave-ice / gourmet
Area: Aina Haina
Price: $
Must Order: lilikoi cream — tangy and rich, their signature; strawberry lemonade with li hing mui powder on top; haupia with azuki beans on the bottom
Parking: Small lot in Aina Haina Shopping Center — easy parking

Founded by Clayton 'Uncle Clay' Chang, who grew up eating shave ice from old-school stands and wanted to recreate that experience using all-natural ingredients. Every syrup is made in-house from real fruit with no artificial colors or flavors -- a mission that won him Honolulu Magazine's Best Shave Ice multiple years running. The Aina Haina strip mall location keeps the crowd mostly local. Uncle Clay and his family work the counter daily, and the 'pure aloha' ethos is genuinely felt.

Title: Waiola Shave Ice
Description:
Honolulu institution since 1979 — cloud-soft ice that soaks up syrup better than anywhere.

Category: shave-ice / classic
Area: Moiliili
Price: $
Must Order: any flavor — the ice texture is the star here; azuki beans; li hing mui
Parking: Street parking on Waiola St or in the small lot behind the shop

Operating in Mo'ili'ili since 1979, Waiola is the shave ice spot that UH Manoa students, professors, and neighborhood families have relied on for over four decades. The ice texture here is legendary -- shaved so fine it's almost powdery, meaning the syrup absorbs completely instead of pooling at the bottom. Won Honolulu Magazine's Best Shave Ice multiple years. The residential neighborhood location means no tourist crowds, just regulars who consider any other shave ice inferior.

Title: Bailey's Antiques & Aloha Shirts
Description:
The world's largest collection of vintage aloha shirts — 15,000+ pieces in a Kapahulu warehouse that's a museum you can shop.

Category: shopping / vintage
Area: Kapahulu
Price: $-$$$
Hours: Daily 10am-6pm
Best Time: Weekday afternoons for unhurried browsing
Parking: Street parking on Kapahulu Ave
Kid Friendly: Yes

Owner Dave Bailey started collecting aloha shirts in the 1970s and turned his obsession into the world's largest vintage aloha shirt collection -- over 15,000 pieces crammed floor to ceiling in a Kapahulu warehouse. Rare 1930s-50s rayon silks from makers like Shaheen and Kamehameha can fetch thousands. Barack Obama, Anthony Bourdain, and Japanese vintage collectors regularly shop here. The $5-$20 rack near the door has legitimate treasures for those willing to dig.

Title: Surf n Sea
Description:
The oldest surf shop in Hawaii (1965) — real surfers sell and rent gear in a colorful Haleiwa waterfront landmark.

Category: shopping / surf-shop
Area: North Shore
Price: $$
Hours: Daily 9am-7pm
Best Time: Morning for the best advice on where to surf that day; check the used board section in back
Parking: Small lot; street parking on Kamehameha Hwy
Kid Friendly: Yes

Founded in 1965 by Dr. Lowell Chenoweth, a dentist-turned-surfer, Surf N Sea is Hawaii's oldest surf shop and a North Shore institution. The colorful wooden building on the Haleiwa waterfront is one of the most photographed structures on the North Shore. The staff are working surfers who can tell you where the best waves are breaking that day. The used board section in back has deals from departing pros, and the shop sponsors the annual Buffalo Big Board Surfing Classic.

Title: Three Tables
Description:
The easiest reef entry on the North Shore — walk right in from sand and float over a turtle-dotted reef.

Category: snorkel / beginner
Area: North Shore
Price: Free
Hours: Open 24 hours (no gates; Pupukea Beach Park)
Best Time: Summer (May–Sep) for calm conditions; morning for best visibility
Parking: Shares free lot with Shark's Cove across the street
Kid Friendly: Yes

Named for three flat reef platforms that emerge at low tide, this site sits within the Pupukea Marine Life Conservation District where fishing and collecting are prohibited — so reef life is noticeably denser than unprotected areas. The sandy channel between the tables is a cleaning station where honu line up to let cleaner wrasse pick parasites off their shells. Winter swells turn this same reef into a violent washing machine — the season shift catches visitors off guard.
